content_analysis.research_question: How to optimize DNA sequencing analysis workflows for large-scale variant discovery to achieve better scalability, portability, and cost-efficiency across various compute infrastructures?
methods: Nextflow DSL2 framework for modular pipeline design
Intra-sample parallelization by sharding FastQ files
Switching internal file formats from BAM to CRAM to reduce storage
Containerization using Docker and Singularity via Biocontainers
Integration of modern tools like fastP, DragMap, and DeepVariant
Cloud benchmarking on AWS Batch using Tower Forge

findings.finding: Achieved a 70% cost reduction in commercial clouds for large-scale data analysis.
findings.evidence: Both [CRAM usage and parallelization] are leading to a 70% cost reduction in commercial clouds...

findings.finding: Using the CRAM format reduced storage usage for the work directory by 65%.
findings.evidence: The storage usage for the work directory for pre-processing these samples drops by 65%, from 170.4 TB (BAM) to 59.7 TB (CRAM).

findings.finding: Compute costs on AWS Batch dropped by 69% compared to the previous version.
findings.evidence: Our findings demonstrate a 69% reduction in compute costs when utilizing the nf-core/sarek 3.1.1 pipeline, in comparison to a previous version, nf-core/sarek 2.7.2.

findings.finding: The median runtime of mapping processes was reduced by 37% through sharding.
findings.evidence: The overall runtime for the mapping processes decreases until it reaches a plateau at 12 shards, achieving a reduction of the median runtime to 37%.

findings.finding: For a single sample, CPU hours were reduced by approximately 70% and runtime by 84%.
findings.evidence: For a single sample the needed CPU hours are reduced by approximately 70%, and the runtime by 84%.

figures.description: A comparative table of various published variant calling pipelines highlighting their workflow languages and supported features like germline or somatic analysis.
An architectural overview of the nf-core/sarek pipeline showing the sequential stages of pre-processing, variant calling, and annotation.
Resource usage comparison (time, CPU, memory, and storage) between BAM and CRAM formats for main pipeline processes.
Graphs showing the impact of sharding input files and parallelizing interval groups on runtime, storage, and CPU consumption.
Performance benchmarking (precision, recall, and F1-score) of different variant callers against ground-truth datasets for germline and somatic variants.
Comparison of copy-number variation calls across tools and patients, categorized by deletions and amplifications.

tables.description: Lists the ICGC LICA-FR cohort datasets used for benchmarking, specifying donor IDs and file characteristics.
Compares average costs, runtime, and CPU hours per patient on AWS Batch between sarek versions 2.7.2 and 3.1.1.

limitations: Switching to CRAM increases memory usage by approximately 34%.
Excessive sharding can increase storage usage because sub-FastQ files are written to disk.
Parallelization benefits plateau; for example, no significant runtime reduction was found beyond 21 interval groups.
Performance of copy-number variation tools varies significantly between different samples.

draft_questions.question: According to the findings, what was the specific reduction in storage usage for the work directory when switching from BAM to CRAM, and what were the corresponding data volumes?
draft_questions.expected_answer: Switching from BAM to CRAM format reduced the storage usage for the work directory by 65%. Specifically, the storage volume dropped from 170.4 TB when using BAM to 59.7 TB when using CRAM.

draft_questions.question: How does the nf-core/sarek 3.1.1 pipeline leverage architectural and methodological changes to optimize cost and performance for large-scale data analysis?
draft_questions.expected_answer: The pipeline achieves significant optimization by transitioning to the Nextflow DSL2 framework, which allows for modular design and intra-sample parallelization through FastQ sharding. By combining these parallelization techniques with a switch from BAM to CRAM internal file formats and the integration of modern tools like DeepVariant, the system realizes a 70% reduction in commercial cloud costs and up to an 84% reduction in runtime for single samples.

draft_questions.question: Based on the benchmarking of mapping processes, what was the numerical impact of input sharding on median runtime, and at what point was the maximum efficiency plateau reached?
draft_questions.expected_answer: Sharding the input files for intra-sample parallelization reduced the median runtime of the mapping processes to 37% of the original time. The benchmarking results show that this reduction in runtime reaches a performance plateau when the input is divided into 12 shards.
